revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Jingūkyō]][[ja:神宮教]][[simple:Jingu-kyo]]


{{Short description|Shinto Sect}}
{{Distinguish|Tenshō Kōtai Jingūkyō}}
{{Infobox organization
| predecessor = [[Ise Grand Shrine]] local administration
| successor = [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]
| parent_organization = {{ill|Institute of Divinities|en|Institute of Divinities|qid=Q11590709|lt=Institute of Divinities|1=Institute of Divinities}} (1940–1946)
}}

{{Nihongo|'''Jingūkyō'''|神宮教}} is a sect of Shinto that originated from [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the Ise faith. It was not technically a [[Sect Shinto]] group but had characteristics of one.<ref name="inoue-2023a">{{Cite encyclopedia |title=Jingūkyō |encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher={{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|qid=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University|1=Kokugakuin University}} |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9812 |last=Inoue |first=Nobutaka |language=en |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230916125955/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9812 |archive-date=2023-09-16 |url-status=live}}</ref> It was founded in 1882, and was reorganized into the {{Nihongo|Jingū Service Foundation|神宮奉斎会|Jingu-hosai-kai}} in 1899.<ref name="inoue-2023a" />

== Overview ==
Jingukyo was established during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} in Japan. While it was not included as one of the thirteen sects of prewar Shinto, it possessed characteristics of sect Shinto until the mid-Meiji period. It was organized by Urata Nagatami and others, with Tanaka Yoritsune as its first Superintendent.<ref name="inoue-2023a" />

In 1872, Urata Nagatami asked for permission to start a Shinto church called Jingū Kyōkai, shortly after the [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] was created. He opened teaching centers and religious groups to organize his disciples, then combined them under the name Divine Wind Religious Association in 1873. Popular pilgrimage groups that were already established were also included. The Jingūkyo Institute started spreading its teachings throughout the country after the dissolution of the Daikyōin in 1875.<ref name="inoue-2023a" />

In 1882, the [[Kyodo Shoku]] was abolished, which led to the Jingūkyo Institute declaring itself independent from the official Grand Shrine Administration Office and renaming itself Jingūkyō. Tanaka Yoritsune became the first superintendent after Urata resigned. Membership increased until the late 1880s, but there was little growth afterward. In 1898, Japan's Civil Law was passed, and there was a movement to make the Jingūkyō a service organization for the Grand Shrines of Ise. As a result, the Jingūkyō was reorganized into the Jingū Hōsaikai in September 1899.<ref name="inoue-2023a" />

== The missionary body of the Ise faith ==
On July 20, 1872 (Meiji 5), he was appointed Chotami Urada, a minor chief priest of [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and also a member of [[:en:Ministry of Religious Education|Ministry of Education]]{{sfn|村上|2007|p={{page needed|date=May 2023}}}} requested the establishment of the {{Nihongo|Jingu Church|神宮教会|Jingū kyōkai}}, and in October, he submitted a notification for the {{Nihongo|Jingu-kyoin|神宮教院|Jingūkyō-in}} for teaching and learning, established a kosha for the followers, and established the existing Ise-kō (Taijōkō) as the foundation of the Jingu Church. The Jingu-kyoin was reorganized under the umbrella of the Jingu Church based on the existing Ise-ko (Taiji-ko).{{Sfn|井上|1991|pp=33–34}} The Jingu-kyoin was established as the center of the Jingu Church.{{Sfn|西川|1988|pp=192–193}} In {{ill|1873|en|1873|qid=Q7757|lt=1873|1=1873}} (the 6th year of Meiji), based on a donation of 2,000 ryo by {{ill|Matsudaira Munehide|en|Matsudaira Munehide|qid=Q3299344|lt=Matsudaira Munehide|1=Matsudaira Munehide}}, {{ill|High priest|en|High priest|qid=Q3114821|lt=Guji|1=High priest}} of [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], a preaching hall was established to serve as a model for shrines throughout Japan, and in August it was named Jiyoukan, which meant "Shrine within Meiji Jingu The church was meant to be a church,{{Sfn|井上|1991|pp=33–34}} the notification was made in March, and the building opened on October 1.{{Sfn|西川|1988|p=194}} The local {{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|qid=Q11632184|lt=koshas|1=Kō (lecture)}} called themselves patriotic {{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|qid=Q11632184|lt=koshas|1=Kō (lecture)}}, etc., but in October of {{ill|1873|en|1873|qid=Q7757|lt=1873|1=1873}} (1873) they were unified and became Kamikaze {{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|qid=Q11632184|lt=Koshas|1=Kō (lecture)}}.{{Sfn|井上|1991|pp=33–34}}

From July to December of 1873, the Jingu Church's pilgrimage was planned by Urata and preached by Honjo and other ministers, and attracted an audience of 9,000 or more people on most days, and several hundred to 2,000 on others.{{Sfn|井上|1991|pp=348–350}}

After the dissolution of the [[Taikyo Institute|Great Teaching Institute]], in accordance with Urada's teaching policy, a branch office was established in Tokyo, and each diocese in Japan had one headquarters church and branch churches.{{Sfn|井上|1991|pp=33–34}}

== Independence as a denominational Shinto sect ==
In 1882, the Ministry of Home Affairs issued the "Ministry of Home Affairs Announcement No. B No. 7 of January 24, 1882," abolishing the dual role of the priest who presided over rituals and the [[Kyodo Shoku]] who conducted proselytizing. Shortly after this, the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the Jingu-kyoin were separated, and the Jingu-kyoin became a branch of the [[Sect Shinto]] called the Shinto Jingu-ha.{{Sfn|井上|1991|pp=33-34}} After Urata retired in {{ill|1877|en|1877|qid=Q7773|lt=1877|1=1877}} (10th year of Meiji), {{ill|Yoritsune Tanaka|ja|田中頼庸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoritsune Tanaka|qq=}}, who had worked on the teaching, became the first head minister.{{Sfn|井上|1991|pp=33-34}} On October 5, the various schools of the Sect Shinto were independent under the name of the school, but were not branches each with the name of the denomination as not being.{{Sfn|菅田|1985|p=177}}

In 1882 (Meiji 15), it was agreed that the production and distribution of the [[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]] would be entrusted to the '''Jingūkyō''' administration, but the following year it was changed so that the Jingu Office was responsible for production and the '''Jingūkyō''' administration was responsible for distribution. {{Sfn|村上|2007|pp=117–118}}

== Tokyo Daijingu ==
{{Main|Tokyo Daijingu}}
In 1882 (Meiji 15), in {{ill|Hibiya|en|Hibiya|qid=Q1134450|lt=Hibiya|1=Hibiya}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, the '''Jingūkyō''' administration built the [[Tokyo Daijingu]], then called ''Hibiya Daijingu''.{{Sfn|村上|2007|pp=117–118}}

After the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Kanto Earthquake]], the shrine was moved to {{ill|Iidabashi|en|Iidabashi|qid=Q5994914|lt=Iidabashi|1=Iidabashi}} in 1928 and renamed to ''Iidabashi Daijingu''. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, the place changed its name to Tokyo Daijingu.<ref>{{Cite web |title=English - Tokyo Daijingu |url=http://www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2021-11-07 |website=www.tokyodaijingu.or.jp |language=ja}}</ref>

== Development and reorganization to the Association of Shinto Shrines through the Jingu-hosai-kai ==
On September 24, 1899, the organization was reorganized as the Jingu-hosai-kai Foundation, an organization of reverends, due to the narrowing of the scope of its activities with the establishment of national Shinto and criticism of leaving the distribution of [[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]], a national project, to one religious sect {{Sfn|村上|2007|pp=117–118}} In accordance with the [[Shinto Directive]] of January 23, 1946, the {{ill|National Association of Shinto Priests|en|National Association of Shinto Priests|qid=Q11389505|lt=National Association of Shinto Priests|1=National Association of Shinto Priests}}, the [[:en:Office of Japanese Classics Research|Office of Japanese Classics Research]], and Jingūkyō took the lead to establish the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].{{Sfn|中山|2009|p=231}}{{Sfn|文化庁編さん|2011|pp=54–61}}

==Present==
There is currently a religious organization of the same name in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} [[:en:Tanba-Sasayama|Tanba Sasayama City]]. It is not a direct successor to the prewar Jingu-kyo, but has the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] branch as its deities<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018-09-19 |script-title=ja:篠山の歴史を知る「篠山町75th year 史」ー「宗教（宗教法人神宮教）」｜篠山市（丹波篠山） |url=https://www.city.sasayama.hyogo.jp/pc/database/75-syukyo05.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180919132526/https://www.city.sasayama.hyogo.jp/pc/database/75-syukyo05.html |archive-date=2018-09-19 |access-date=2022-05-04 |website=Sasayama City}}</ref> and the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and is headed by Harufumi Hioki, the chief priest of [[Ikuta Shrine]], a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrine]], and is not unrelated to [[Ise Grand Shrine]] or the Shinto Shrines Agency.

== Officials ==

*  {{ill|Yoritsune Tanaka|ja|田中頼庸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoritsune Tanaka|qq=}} is the grand priest of Ise Jingu Shrine.{{Sfn|井上|1991|pp=33–34}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}
<references group=""></references>

== Bibliography ==

* {{Cite book |last=井上 |first=順孝 |script-title=ja:教派神道の形成 |date=April 1991 |publisher=弘文堂 |isbn=978-4335160219 |author-link=井上順孝}}
* {{Cite book |last=菅田 |first=正昭 |script-title=ja:古神道は甦る |publisher=たま出版 |year=1985 |isbn=4884811321 |author-link=菅田正昭}}（文庫：1994th year .ISBN 4886924603.）
* {{Cite book |last=西川 |first=順土 |script-title=ja:近代の神宮 |publisher=神宮司庁 |year=1988 |series=神宮教養叢書第9 }}.
* {{Cite book |last=村上 |first=重良 |script-title=ja:天皇制国家と宗教 |date=August 2007 |publisher=講談社 |isbn=978-4061598324 |series=講談社学術文庫 |author-link=村上重良}}
* {{Cite book |last=文化庁編さん |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/shukyouhoujin/nenkan/pdf/h21nenkan.pdf |script-title=ja:宗教年鑑 |date=February 2011 |publisher=ぎょうせい |isbn=978-4324091975 |edition=平成21st year 版 |access-date=2022-05-03 |archive-date=2013-01-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130124225937/http://www.bunka.go.jp///shukyouhoujin/nenkan/pdf/h21nenkan.pdf |url-status=dead }}
* {{Cite book |last=中山 |first=郁 |script-title=ja:史料から見た神道-國學院大學の学術資産を中心に |date=March 2009 |publisher=弘文堂 |isbn=978-4335160561 |pages=227–247 |chapter=國學院大學と教派神道 }}
